title: Protecting Higher Education from the Chinese Communist Party Act of 2025
short_title: Protecting Higher Education from the Chinese Communist Party Act of 2025
stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.
plain_summary: This bill stops members of the Chinese Communist Party from getting student visas to study in America or join exchange programs. It also blocks their family members like spouses, children, parents, brothers, sisters, grandchildren, nieces, and nephews from getting these same visas. The President can still let them come if it helps keep America safe or if we have to follow agreements with other countries like the United Nations. The goal is to limit how many Chinese Communist Party members can come to American schools and universities.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Protecting Higher Education from the Chinese Communist Party Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2  Ineligibility for Certain Visas of Members of the Chinese Communist Party
sections.text: People who belong to the Chinese Communist Party cannot get F or J visas to come to the United States. F visas are for students who want to study at American schools. J visas are for people who want to participate in exchange programs. This rule applies to any member of the Chinese Communist Party, including those who have served on the National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party. It also applies to their family members. Family members include a person's spouse, child, parent, brother or sister, grandchild, niece, or nephew. There are two exceptions to this rule. First, if someone needs to come to the United States because of our agreement with the United Nations about their headquarters in New York, they can still get a visa. Second, the President can give someone a waiver if keeping them out would hurt America's national security. The President must write a letter to Congress explaining why the waiver is needed for national security.
